project.name: miStudio
project.description: End-to-end mechanistic interpretability platform: dataset ingestion → SAE training → feature extraction → LLM labeling → model steering

nodes.name: encryption.py
nodes.summary: Provides AES-256-GCM envelope encryption and decryption for sensitive application settings (API keys, secrets), using HKDF-SHA256 key derivation from the configured SECRET_KEY.

nodes.languageNotes: Uses nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:alpine for the final stage rather than the standard nginx image, satisfying Docker Scout's non-root container policy without a custom USER instruction; the nginx.conf is written inline via heredoc, embedding SPA routing and gzip compression configuration directly in the Dockerfile.

nodes.name: README-GCP.md
nodes.summary: Comprehensive GCP deployment guide covering prerequisites (NVIDIA T4, Docker, Container Toolkit), quick-start via setup-gcp.sh, manual deployment steps, service access URLs, data persistence, and troubleshooting for GPU, WebSocket, and database issues.

nodes.name: docker-compose.gcp.yml
nodes.summary: GCP production Docker Compose manifest orchestrating 9 services — miStudio backend/frontend, Celery worker and beat, PostgreSQL, Neuronpedia PostgreSQL (pgvector), Redis, Ollama (local LLM), and Nginx reverse proxy — with GPU reservations, health checks, and named volumes.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses NVIDIA GPU device reservations on backend, celery-worker, and ollama services; requires both MISTUDIO_POSTGRES_PASSWORD and NEURONPEDIA_POSTGRES_PASSWORD secrets as mandatory env vars.

nodes.name: postgres
nodes.summary: PostgreSQL 15 instance for miStudio application data with health-check and named volume persistence.

nodes.name: neuronpedia-postgres
nodes.summary: pgvector-enabled PostgreSQL 15 instance for the co-deployed local Neuronpedia instance.

nodes.name: redis
nodes.summary: Redis 7 Alpine instance acting as Celery broker and result backend with persistence volume.

nodes.name: backend
nodes.summary: miStudio FastAPI backend container with GPU reservation, wiring to Postgres, Redis, and Ollama, and Neuronpedia local integration env vars.

nodes.name: celery-worker
nodes.summary: GPU-enabled Celery worker container running background tasks (training, extraction, labeling) with the same image as the backend.

nodes.name: celery-beat
nodes.summary: Celery Beat scheduler container for periodic tasks (system monitoring emissions, etc.) using the backend image.

nodes.name: frontend
nodes.summary: miStudio React frontend served via nginx-unprivileged on port 8080 (exposed as 3000), depends on backend.

nodes.name: neuronpedia-webapp
nodes.summary: Local Neuronpedia Next.js webapp container for sharing and browsing SAE features, backed by neuronpedia-postgres.

nodes.name: ollama
nodes.summary: Ollama local LLM inference server with GPU access, used by the backend for local labeling via OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

nodes.name: nginx
nodes.summary: Nginx Alpine reverse proxy on port 80 routing traffic to backend, frontend, and Neuronpedia webapp using a GCP-specific nginx config.

nodes.name: setup-gcp.sh
nodes.summary: One-shot GCP instance provisioning script that installs Docker, Docker Compose plugin, and NVIDIA Container Toolkit if absent, verifies GPU Docker access, copies compose files to /opt/mistudio, and starts all services.

nodes.name: FEATURE_ANALYSIS_PROMPT.txt
nodes.summary: Short reference prompt template for manual SAE feature analysis, providing a skeleton system/user message structure for asking an LLM to identify the semantic pattern shared across a set of activation examples.

nodes.name: HuggingFace_Progress_API_Gaps.md
nodes.summary: Technical analysis documenting HuggingFace library limitations around real-time progress reporting, the three workarounds implemented in miStudio (DownloadProgressMonitor, tqdm WebSocket bridge, hardcoded checkpoint emissions), and four upstream improvement requests.

nodes.name: miStudio_Manual.md
nodes.summary: Comprehensive 599-line user manual covering all five major workflow stages — data/model management, SAE training (6 frameworks), feature extraction, auto-labeling, and model steering — with hardware requirements, configuration reference, advanced usage, and a troubleshooting appendix.

nodes.name: index.html
nodes.summary: Root HTML entry point for the Vite-based React SPA; mounts the `#root` div and bootstraps the module graph via `src/main.tsx`.

nodes.name: package.json
nodes.summary: Frontend package manifest for mistudio-frontend v0.5.0 defining React 18, Zustand, Socket.IO-client, Recharts, and react-markdown runtime dependencies alongside Vite/Vitest/TypeScript tooling and dev scripts.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses Vite as bundler with vitest for unit testing; `type: module` enables ES module resolution throughout.

nodes.name: tsconfig.json
nodes.summary: Main TypeScript compiler configuration for the frontend enabling strict mode, bundler module resolution, react-jsx transform, and `@/*` path alias mapping to `./src/*`.
nodes.languageNotes: Full strict suite enabled (noImplicitAny, strictNullChecks, strictFunctionTypes, etc.) with `noEmit` since Vite handles transpilation; excludes test files so tsconfig.test.json handles them separately.

nodes.name: tsconfig.node.json
nodes.summary: Supplemental TypeScript configuration targeting the Vite config file (`vite.config.ts`) with Node.js-compatible compiler settings and ESNext module resolution.

nodes.name: tsconfig.test.json
nodes.summary: TypeScript configuration extending the main tsconfig for the Vitest test environment, adding `src/test/**/*` to the include list so test utilities and helpers are covered.

nodes.name: mistudio-deployment.yaml
nodes.summary: Complete Kubernetes manifest for the `mistudio` namespace deploying all application components: PostgreSQL, Redis, FastAPI backend (with co-located Celery worker and beat scheduler in the GPU pod), nginx-unprivileged frontend, Ollama ExternalName proxy, and dual Ingress rules for HTTP and WebSocket traffic.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses Recreate strategy for the GPU backend pod (required for single-GPU workloads), hostPath volumes for persistence, and separate Ingress resources for HTTP, WebSocket, and Ollama path rewriting.

nodes.name: mistudio-secrets.yaml.example
nodes.summary: Example Kubernetes Secret template for the `mistudio` namespace documenting how to generate and apply the four required secrets (postgres-password, database-url, database-url-sync, secret-key) with base64-encoded values.

nodes.name: neuronpedia-deployment.yaml
nodes.summary: Kubernetes manifest deploying a local Neuronpedia instance in a dedicated `neuronpedia` namespace, including a PostgreSQL StatefulSet, Prisma migration init container using a custom migrator image, the Next.js webapp Deployment, and an Ingress for `k8s-neuron.hitsai.local`.
nodes.languageNotes: Prisma schema migrations are handled via an init container (hitsai/neuronpedia-migrator) so the standalone Next.js image does not need Prisma CLI at runtime.

nodes.name: neuronpedia-migrator.Dockerfile
nodes.summary: Dockerfile building a Node 20 Alpine image that retains Prisma CLI and a generated Prisma client, used as a Kubernetes init container to run `prisma db push` before the Neuronpedia webapp starts.

nodes.name: backup-db.sh
nodes.summary: Shell script that dumps the `mistudio` PostgreSQL database via `pg_dump` inside the Docker container, compresses the output with gzip, and retains only the most recent 10 timestamped backup files.

nodes.name: k8s-helpers.sh
nodes.summary: Sourceable Bash library of 26 helper functions for operating the miStudio, Neuronpedia, and miLLM deployments on a remote Kubernetes host over SSH, covering image-readiness checks, rollout, log inspection, schema migration, and GPU monitoring.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses sshpass for non-interactive SSH to the K8s host; each helper wraps a `kubectl exec` or `kubectl rollout` command, making it safe to source and call from CI or developer shells.

nodes.name: k8s_deploy
nodes.summary: Full rolling deployment of the mistudio backend: pulls latest images on the K8s host, triggers a rollout restart, waits for rollout completion, and verifies pod status.

nodes.name: np_deploy
nodes.summary: Full rolling deployment of the Neuronpedia service: pulls latest images, restarts the deployment in the neuronpedia namespace, and waits for rollout.

nodes.name: millm_deploy
nodes.summary: Full rolling deployment of the miLLM service on the K8s host, following the same pull-restart-wait pattern as k8s_deploy and np_deploy.

nodes.name: np_purge
nodes.summary: Destructively purges Neuronpedia database data by running SQL DELETE statements against the neuronpedia-postgres pod; intended for development environment resets.

nodes.name: restore-db.sh
nodes.summary: Shell script that drops and recreates the `mistudio` database in the Docker container then restores from a `.sql` or `.sql.gz` backup file after interactive confirmation.

nodes.name: start-workers.sh
nodes.summary: Starts Celery workers for local development with four named profiles (minimal, full, datasets, training) each spawning workers on appropriate queues with tuned concurrency and memory limits.

nodes.name: start_worker
nodes.summary: Launches a named Celery worker process in the background with specified queues, concurrency, and optional extra arguments; writes a PID file for later graceful shutdown.

nodes.name: stop-workers.sh
nodes.summary: Stops all Celery workers previously started by `start-workers.sh` by reading PID files, sending SIGTERM with a 10-second graceful shutdown window, and force-killing any survivors.

nodes.name: install-services.sh
nodes.summary: Shell script for installing, uninstalling, checking status, and tailing logs of all MiStudio systemd services; validates prerequisites (Docker, virtualenv) and copies .service/.target files to /etc/systemd/system/.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses set -e for fail-fast behavior and requires sudo for systemctl operations.

nodes.name: mistudio-backend.service
nodes.summary: Systemd service unit for the MiStudio FastAPI backend, running uvicorn on port 8000 with auto-restart, Wants on Redis/PostgreSQL/Celery worker, and journald logging.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses Wants= (soft dependency) for Redis and PostgreSQL since those run in Docker Compose, not systemd.

nodes.name: mistudio-celery-beat.service
nodes.summary: Systemd service unit for the MiStudio Celery Beat scheduler, managing periodic tasks (system monitoring, cleanup) with auto-restart and dependency on the Celery worker service.

nodes.name: mistudio-celery-worker.service
nodes.summary: Systemd service unit for the MiStudio Celery worker, using --pool=solo (required for CUDA/GPU tasks) and consuming high_priority, datasets, training, extraction and other queues with graceful 120-second stop timeout.
nodes.languageNotes: --pool=solo is mandatory to prevent fork() breaking CUDA initialization in GPU tasks.

nodes.name: mistudio.target
nodes.summary: Systemd target that groups all MiStudio application services (backend, celery-worker, celery-beat) for unified start/stop management via a single systemctl command.

nodes.name: mistudio
nodes.summary: Unified top-level start/stop control script with four subcommands (dev, prod, stop, status) that delegates to `start-mistudio.sh`, `start-docker.sh`, or direct process management depending on the selected mode.
nodes.languageNotes: Shell script without a `.sh` extension intended to be invoked as `./mistudio <cmd>`; defines named functions (show_usage, start_dev, start_prod, stop_all, show_status) dispatched via a case statement.

nodes.name: nginx.conf
nodes.summary: Nginx reverse-proxy configuration for native development mode routing traffic on port 80 to the Vite frontend (host:3000), FastAPI backend (host:8000), Socket.IO WebSocket endpoint, Ollama LLM proxy, and a separate server block for the Neuronpedia webapp; includes dynamic CORS origin mapping.
nodes.languageNotes: Blocks `/api/internal/` before `/api/` (longest-prefix-match order) to prevent internal Celery endpoints from being externally accessible; uses `map` for CORS origin whitelisting and WebSocket upgrade handling.

nodes.name: nginx.docker.conf
nodes.summary: Nginx configuration for Docker Compose production mode using Docker service-name upstreams (backend:8000, frontend:8080) with fixed CORS origin for `dkr-mistudio.hitsai.local`, WebSocket upgrade support, and a `/health` liveness endpoint.
nodes.languageNotes: References frontend on port 8080 (nginx-unprivileged image does not bind port 80); uses Docker's internal DNS resolver `127.0.0.11` for service discovery.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand what miStudio is: an end-to-end mechanistic interpretability platform that takes you from raw datasets, through sparse autoencoder (SAE) training, into feature extraction, LLM-based labeling, and finally model steering. This document lays out the core pipeline stages and the tech stack (FastAPI + PyTorch backend, React + Zustand frontend, Celery task queue), giving you the mental map for every step that follows.

tour.title: Backend Entry Point
tour.description: backend/src/main.py is the FastAPI application that bootstraps the entire backend: it wires up the v1 API routers, Socket.IO for real-time updates, middleware, and lifespan startup. This is the runtime root of the dependency graph, so the modules it pulls in (config, database, routers) define the backend's architecture. Everything the server can do begins here.
tour.languageLesson: FastAPI uses an ASGI lifespan context manager to run startup/shutdown logic, and its dependency-injection system (Depends) resolves shared resources like DB sessions per request without global state.

tour.title: Core Foundations: Config & Database
tour.description: config.py and database.py are the two most depended-upon modules in the whole codebase (fan-in of 58 and 48). config.py centralizes all environment settings, GPU paths, and encrypted secrets, while database.py defines the async SQLAlchemy engine and session factory used by every service and worker. Understanding these first explains how nearly every downstream module gets its settings and data access.
tour.languageLesson: SQLAlchemy's async engine yields sessions through an async generator dependency; the session is created per request and automatically closed, preventing connection leaks under concurrency.

tour.title: The Data Model
tour.description: These SQLAlchemy ORM models are the highest fan-in domain entities and define the vocabulary the whole platform speaks: Model (the transformer under study), Dataset (the corpus), Training (an SAE training run), and Feature (a discovered interpretable direction). Every service, worker, and API endpoint reads and writes these tables, so learning the relationships here — a Dataset feeds a Training, a Training produces Features — reveals the shape of the entire pipeline.

tour.title: Database Schema & Migrations
tour.description: The Alembic migration files define the actual Postgres schema behind the models from Step 4, applied incrementally as the platform evolved. The datasets and training-related tables map directly onto the ORM entities you just saw, and later migrations add tables for activation extractions and enhanced labeling jobs — mirroring the pipeline stages ahead. This is the persistent backbone that survives across training runs and restarts.
tour.languageLesson: Alembic migrations are ordered and should be idempotent and reversible; each file applies incremental DDL so the database schema can evolve in lockstep with the application code.

tour.title: Stage 1 — Dataset Ingestion
tour.description: The pipeline begins with data. dataset_service.py handles CRUD and deep-merge metadata for datasets, while tokenization_service.py analyzes a dataset's schema, preprocesses, and tokenizes it into the format SAE training consumes. Building on the Dataset model from Step 4, these services turn a HuggingFace dataset or local corpus into tokenized activations-ready input — the raw material for everything downstream.

tour.title: Stage 2 — SAE Training Core
tour.description: This is the ML heart of the project. sae_architectures.py implements six paper-grounded sparse autoencoder variants (Standard, JumpReLU, TopK, and more), sae_training.py runs the training loop with sparsity losses and dead-neuron detection, and layer_discovery.py introspects any transformer architecture dynamically so no model needs hardcoded support. Together they train an SAE on the tokenized activations from Step 6, producing the interpretable features the rest of the platform analyzes.
tour.languageLesson: layer_discovery uses runtime introspection (walking the model's module tree) instead of if/elif architecture whitelists, so any HuggingFace transformer can be steered or extracted from without code changes — a form of duck-typed structural discovery.

tour.title: The Task Queue: Async Execution
tour.description: SAE training and extraction are long-running GPU jobs, so they never block the API — they run as Celery tasks. base_task.py provides the shared base with DB sessions and error handling, training_tasks.py drives the training loop from Step 7 asynchronously, and websocket_emitter.py streams live progress back to the browser. This async, WebSocket-first architecture is the connective tissue that makes every heavy pipeline stage feel real-time in the UI.
tour.languageLesson: Celery decouples request handling from execution: the API enqueues a task and returns immediately, while a separate worker process runs it. Progress is pushed to clients via Socket.IO rooms rather than polling.

tour.title: Stage 3 — Feature Extraction
tour.description: Once an SAE is trained, extraction_service.py orchestrates running it over the corpus to extract per-feature activations, and activation_service.py manages the full activation extraction pipeline that feeds it. This stage populates the feature-activation records that make each learned direction inspectable — the bridge between an abstract trained SAE and concrete, browsable interpretable features.

tour.title: Stage 4 — LLM Feature Labeling
tour.description: Extracted features are just numbered directions until they get human-readable names. labeling_service.py orchestrates bulk labeling, while enhanced_labeling_service.py runs a sophisticated two-pass per-feature process — summarizing each activating example, then synthesizing a shared semantic pattern — using the OpenAI-compatible SDK. This stage turns raw activations from Step 9 into interpretable descriptions that make the whole platform useful to a human analyst.

tour.title: Stage 5 — Model Steering
tour.description: The payoff of interpretability: steering_service.py is the mechanistic-steering engine that loads a model and SAE, then injects or ablates specific feature directions during generation to change model behavior in a controlled, comparative way. It relies on the same dynamic layer discovery from Step 7 to hook into any architecture. This closes the loop — the labeled features from Step 10 become levers you can pull to steer the model.

tour.title: The Frontend Application
tour.description: The React frontend mirrors the backend pipeline as a set of panels. App.tsx composes the sidebar-navigated panels (Datasets, Training, SAEs, Features, Steering, System Monitor), and client.ts is the shared Axios instance that all API modules use to talk to the backend from Step 2. Zustand stores subscribe to the WebSocket channels emitted in Step 8, giving the UI live progress with automatic HTTP-polling fallback.
tour.languageLesson: The frontend uses a WebSocket-first pattern: Zustand stores update from Socket.IO events, and fall back to HTTP polling only when the socket disconnects, so the UI stays live without redundant network chatter.

tour.title: Containerization, Orchestration & CI/CD
tour.description: Finally, see how it all ships. The backend Dockerfile packages the FastAPI app and PyTorch dependencies, docker-compose.yml orchestrates the full stack — Postgres, Redis, backend, Celery worker, Celery beat, frontend, and Nginx — as the services referenced throughout this tour, and the docker-images GitHub Actions workflow builds and publishes those images on every push to main for K8s deployment. This is how the pipeline you just learned becomes a running production system.
tour.languageLesson: docker-compose defines multi-service dependency ordering with depends_on and named volumes for persistent Postgres/Redis data; CI builds the images once and Kubernetes Deployments then roll them out with replica management and rolling updates.
